cycles.name: Foundation Forge
focuses: Build baseline strength in compound lifts and establish progressive core overload.
Ingrain movement quality, lock in training habits, and initiate a sustainable caloric deficit.

cycles.name: Volume Engine
cycles.tagline: More Work. Less Rest. Real Growth.
focuses: Increase hypertrophy volume per muscle group with higher training density and shorter rest periods.
Advance core loading from bodyweight to stability-based variations and introduce structured conditioning blocks.

cycles.name: Metabolic Override
focuses: Shift training toward high-density supersets and circuit-based resistance work to maximise energy expenditure while preserving muscle.
Peak direct abdominal volume and introduce advanced anti-extension progressions.

paragraphs: Cycle 2 did its job: bench up 25%, shoulder press up 21%, and three straight perfect weeks to finish. You're stronger and you've proven you can handle density. But the scale didn't move — and the goal was never just to be a bigger 88 kg. Cycle 3 is where that changes.

Metabolic Override turns the volume you built into fuel. Every session now runs on supersets — two exercises back to back, short rests, no dead time. The weights come down slightly and the pace goes up; that's not a compromise, it's the design. You'll burn more, keep every gram of muscle, and your conditioning finishers get sharper teeth.
Your job: respect the rest clock, log every set, and let the nutrition finally match the training. This is the cycle where the work becomes visible.
outcomes: Weekly weight average trending down 0.3–0.5 kg
Every superset completed inside the prescribed rest — no clock-stretching
Lat pulldown moving again after a six-week plateau
Pain-free lower body all cycle, split squat depth growing week by week
12 direct ab sets per week handled — your highest core dose yet
Conditioning circuit at 4 rounds feeling routine by week 5

cycles.name: Strength Reclaim
focuses: Restore and surpass Cycle 1 strength levels with reduced volume and higher intensity to protect lean mass through the deepening deficit.
Reduce conditioning volume, refine muscular detail through isolation and contraction-focused work.

paragraphs: After this cycle strips the fat, Cycle 4 flips the script. The rests get long again, the bar gets heavy again, and every number you set back in Cycle 1 goes on the table to be beaten — at a lighter, leaner bodyweight.
Less of you, lifting more than ever.

cycles.name: The Reveal
focuses: Maintain peak strength, reduce fatigue accumulation, and bring body composition to target condition.
Transition to a maintenance caloric intake; stress-test the physique and habits that will sustain results long-term.

days.completionMessage: Four supersets deep with the clock in charge — that's more upper-body work per minute than any session in Cycle 2. Eat your protein tonight; the pace only builds from here.

good: Smooth circles, build the pace gently through the five minutes
Feel the whole body warm and the breathing lift
bad: Racing it — this is a raise, not a workout

good: Hands finish wide, thumbs pointing behind you
Feel the squeeze between the shoulder blades
bad: Shrugging the traps up toward the ears

good: Shoulders drop away from the ears, body hangs long
Feel the lats stretch and the spine decompress
bad: Bending the elbows or swinging

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bench Press
exercises.note: Start with the 20 kg dumbbells you finished Cycle 2 on. At 60-second rests they'll feel heavier than you remember — if the RPE creeps past 8, step down a pair instead of stretching the rest.

good: Press the dumbbells up and slightly toward each other at the top
Feel the stretch across the chest at the bottom, then squeeze the pecs to drive the bells up
bad: Flaring the elbows to 90 degrees — keep them at roughly 45 degrees to the torso

good: Elbows drive past the ribcage, dumbbells finish beside the hip
Feel the mid-back muscles contract hard at the top
bad: Jerking the weight up with momentum or shrugging

good: Press the handles up and slightly together, following the machine's path
Feel the upper chest lengthen at the bottom, then contract to drive up
bad: Letting the shoulders roll forward off the pad at the bottom

good: Open the arms wide in a smooth arc and lightly draw the shoulder blades together
Feel the rear delts drive the movement, not the upper traps
bad: Bending the elbows through the rep and turning it into a row

good: Arms rise to shoulder height, slight forward lean of the torso
Feel the middle deltoid lift the weight, not the traps
bad: Shrugging the shoulders or swinging the dumbbells

good: Curl the ribcage down toward the pelvis while the hips stay still
Feel the abs shorten with every rep, not the arms pulling
bad: Pulling with the arms or hinging at the hips

days.completionMessage: The one day the rest stays long and the load stays serious — hack squat and leg press carrying your leg strength forward, completely on your terms. Pain-free and progressing is exactly the plan.

good: Smooth circles, build the pace gently through the five minutes
Feel the legs flush warm before the first squat
bad: Racing it — this is a raise, not a workout

good: Hips rise until the body forms a straight line from knees to shoulders
Feel the glutes squeeze hard at the top, not the lower back
bad: Hyperextending the lumbar spine at lockout

exercises.name: Split Squat
exercises.note: The knee's comeback drill. Shallow, slow, zero pain — go a little deeper each week it feels good. If the knee complains, skip it and mention it in your session note.

good: Short stance, lower only as far as stays completely pain-free
Feel the front leg do the work — the back leg is just a kickstand
bad: Pushing through any knee discomfort — depth is earned, not forced

exercises.name: Machine Hack Squat
exercises.note: Your last log showed 40 kg with no RPE next to it. In week one, work up from 35 kg and let the RPE decide — wherever 12 clean reps lands at RPE 7–8 is your working weight.

good: Drive the platform up by pushing through the middle of the feet
Feel the quads lengthen at the bottom, then contract hard as you extend
bad: Letting the knees cave inward as you descend — track them over the middle toes

exercises.name: Machine Leg Press
exercises.note: Start from the weight we used in our July 12 session — strong and completely pain-free. It should sit at RPE 7–8 for 15 reps; if it's easier than that, go up.

good: Feet shoulder-width apart on the platform, knees track over toes
Feel the quads working through the full range
bad: Letting the lower back lift off the pad at the bottom

good: Pad pulls toward the glutes, thighs stay pressed into the seat
Feel the hamstrings contract from behind the knee upward
bad: Lifting the hips off the seat to generate momentum

good: Heels drop below the platform, then rise as high as possible
Feel the soleus load under the knee as the heel lifts
bad: Bouncing at the bottom without pausing

good: From a forearm plank, shift the whole body back a few centimetres, then pull forward
Feel the deep abs brace harder the further back you drift
bad: Letting the hips sag or pike as you slide

days.completionMessage: New grip, new pace, and intervals to finish — the pulldown that sat still for six weeks just got a new problem to solve. Hydrate hard tonight.

good: Smooth circles, build the pace gently through the five minutes
Feel the whole body warm and the breathing lift
bad: Racing it — this is a raise, not a workout

good: Forearm rotates outward while the elbow stays pinned to the ribs
Feel the deep rotator cuff muscles behind the shoulder working
bad: Letting the elbow drift away from the body

good: Wide grip, take the band over the head and behind in one smooth arc
Feel the chest and the front of the shoulders open
bad: Bending the elbows to cheat the arc — go wider instead

exercises.name: Lat Pulldown
exercises.note: A deliberate reset after six stuck weeks. With the neutral grip, start around 42–44 kg — lighter than the 47 you were parked at. Week one should feel smooth; that's the plan, not a mistake.

good: Bar pulls to the upper chest, elbows drive down and slightly back
Feel the lats stretch at the top and contract hard at the bottom
bad: Leaning too far back or pulling with the biceps

good: Press the handles straight up along the line of the machine
Feel the contraction across the top of the shoulders without crunching the neck
bad: Shrugging the shoulders up toward the ears as you press

good: Hands meet at chest height with a slight bend in the elbows throughout
Feel the pec fibres shorten as the arms come together
bad: Bending the elbows more as the weight gets heavy

good: Drive the elbow back past the ribs, hips square to the stack
Feel one side of the mid-back contract fully on every rep
bad: Rotating the torso to yank the weight back

good: Palms face each other, curl to shoulder height with the elbows pinned
Feel the outer bicep and forearm take the load
bad: Swinging the elbows forward to lift the weight

good: Press the bar to full lockout with the elbows glued to the sides
Feel the back of the arm squeeze hard at the bottom
bad: Leaning over the cable and pressing with bodyweight

good: Knees rise above hip height, body stays still from the shoulders up
Feel the lower abdominals curl the pelvis upward
bad: Swinging or using momentum to lift the knees

good: 30 seconds hard, 30 seconds easy — eight rounds, no skipping the hard halves
Feel the legs flood on the hard half and flush on the easy half
bad: Pacing so evenly that the hard 30 seconds stops being hard

days.completionMessage: Four rounds of the circuit on top of a full posterior session — the biggest burn of your week, done. Recover properly: water, protein, sleep. You've earned all three.

good: Smooth circles, build the pace gently through the five minutes
Feel the legs flush warm before the first hinge
bad: Racing it — this is a raise, not a workout

good: Hips rise until the body forms a straight line from knees to shoulders
Feel the glutes squeeze hard at the top, not the lower back
bad: Hyperextending the lumbar spine at lockout

exercises.name: Split Squat
exercises.note: The knee's comeback drill. Shallow, slow, zero pain — go a little deeper each week it feels good. If the knee complains, skip it and mention it in your session note.

good: Short stance, lower only as far as stays completely pain-free
Feel the front leg do the work — the back leg is just a kickstand
bad: Pushing through any knee discomfort — depth is earned, not forced

good: Hinge from the hips and lower the torso toward the floor with a flat back
Feel the stretch through the hamstrings and glutes at the bottom, then squeeze the glutes to come up
bad: Rounding the lower back at the bottom or hyperextending at the top

good: Shin pad reaches full knee extension at the top
Feel the quad — especially just above the kneecap — contract hard
bad: Using momentum to swing the weight up

good: Heels curl toward the glutes while the hips stay pressed into the pad
Feel the hamstrings do the pulling from behind the knee
bad: Lifting the hips off the pad to cheat the weight up

good: Rotate through the trunk and pivot the back foot as you chop across the body
Feel the obliques drive the rotation while the arms stay long
bad: Pulling with the arms instead of rotating through the core

good: Bell reaches chest height, hips snap forward
Feel the glutes and hamstrings drive the bell
bad: Squatting the swing or pulling with the shoulders

good: Ball reaches full overhead, slams into the floor with maximum force
Feel the core contract violently as the ball accelerates down
bad: Bending only at the waist — use the whole body

good: Tall posture, shoulders packed down, no leaning to either side
Feel the grip, core, and upper back working to stabilise the load
bad: Letting the shoulders round forward or the head drop

cards.title: The Scale Has to Move
cards.body:
Honest talk: through Cycle 2 your weight stayed at 88 kg — the deficit we discussed never actually happened. Training was excellent; nutrition is now the single biggest thing between you and the goal.

  • Protein: 160–175 g every day
  • Weekly weight average: trending down 0.3–0.5 kg
  • Flat after two weeks? Cut 200–300 kcal from daily intake and hold.

This cycle burns more than any before it, but no training phase can out-run intake — the deficit is your job, the muscle protection is mine.

cards.title: Water Up, Sugar Down
cards.body:
Following up on your blood work: more water every day, less sugar, and book the re-test — don't let it slide to next cycle.

It matters double now: Metabolic Override makes you sweat more than anything you've done so far. Take a bottle to every session and finish it. Simple, boring, non-negotiable.

cards.title: The Knee Plan
cards.body:
After the lunge pain, all your main leg work this cycle is machine-based and two-legged — hack squat and leg press, both already proven pain-free at full load.

Single-leg work survives only as the shallow split squat in your warm-up: six easy reps, only as deep as stays completely pain-free. Each week it feels good, go slightly deeper — that's how single-leg strength gets earned back.

What's OK: normal muscle burn and next-day stiffness — keep training through it. What's not OK: sharp pain, pain that builds through a set, swelling, or anything unfamiliar in the knee.

  • Mild, and it settles by next session: finish the set, note it in your log.
  • Building, or changing how you move: stop that exercise, use the fallback, and message me the same day — not at the next check-in.
  • Sharp, swollen, or genuinely unfamiliar: stop the whole session and message me before you train again — don't wait it out.

cards.title: Lighter and Faster Is the Point
cards.body:
Supersets with 45–90 second rests mean you cannot lift your Cycle 2 weights — and you're not supposed to. The stimulus this cycle is total work per minute, not load on the bar.

If you catch yourself stretching rest periods to protect a number, you're training the wrong quality. Drop the weight, respect the clock, own the pace.

cards.title: The Pulldown Reset
cards.body:
Your lat pulldown hasn't moved in six weeks — a genuine plateau, so we're breaking it deliberately: new neutral grip, new rep target, lighter start (your exact starting point is on the exercise card as a Coach's Note).

A plateau breaks by changing the stimulus, not by pulling harder at the same wall. If your logs show it climbing by week 3, we ride it; if not, I swap the exercise. Either way, it moves.

cards.title: The RPE Is the Prescription
cards.body:
Your only job on load is the RPE: pick the weight that puts you at the prescribed RPE inside the prescribed rest, and log every set honestly.

  • Set lands below the target RPE → go heavier next set.
  • Set blows past it → back off.

I read your logs every week and adjust the program when they tell me something — you never need to run your own progression math. What you record now also builds your Cycle 4 strength baselines; the log is the whole game.

cards.title: Peak Ab Season
cards.body:
Twelve direct ab sets a week — cable crunch, hanging knee raises, body saw, wood chops — is the highest core dose of your programme, timed exactly when the fat over them is coming off.

The body saw is the new test: drift back only as far as your lower back can stay locked flat.

This dose is deliberately maximal — add nothing on rest days. More isn't better here; it's just recovery debt.

cards.title: The Energy Budget Still Rules
cards.body:
Four gym sessions plus one tennis session — that ceiling stays. It matters more now, not less: you'll be in a real deficit while training denser than ever.

The conditioning you need is already built into Days 3 and 4 — do not add runs or extra cardio on top. Remember Cycle 1's near-fainting episode: that was total demand exceeding recovery.

On low-readiness days, drop every RPE by 1 — and the finisher is always the first thing to cut, never the first lift.
